title: Adventures and Campaigns
description: Structure and organization of D&D gameplay experiences

game_sessions.description: Each time you sit down to play D&D

one_shot.description: Sometimes a session of D&D is a complete game in itself (often called a 'one-shot'), where you play a short adventure that lasts for just one session.

ongoing.description: More often, though, D&D sessions are connected in a longer adventure that takes several sessions to complete, and adventures can be strung together into a larger narrative called a campaign.

adventures.description: An adventure features a fantastic setting and includes a cast of nonplayer characters controlled by the DM.

setting.description: A fantastic setting, such as an underground dungeon, a wondrous wilderness, or a magic-filled city

npcs.description: A cast of nonplayer characters controlled by the DM
npcs.villain: Often one of the NPCs is a villain whose agenda drives much of an adventure's action

content.exploration: The adventurers explore environments, events, and creatures presented by the DM

short_adventure.duration: Take only one session to complete

long_adventure.challenges: Many combats, interactions, and other challenges

campaigns.description: A campaign is a series of adventures with a consistent group of adventurers following the narrative.

analogy.adventure: Like a season of the series
analogy.session: Like a single episode—it might be self-contained, but it usually links to the larger plot if there is one

episodic.description: Some campaigns are episodic, where each adventure is its own tale and not much besides the player characters links it to the next one.

long_running.description: Other campaigns involve long-running plots, a cast of recurring NPCs, and themes that span multiple adventures, leading toward a climactic conclusion.

approaches: DM creates a campaign from scratch
Assemble a campaign from published adventures
Mix homemade material with published material
